questions.question_html:
A deterministic finite automation (DFA) D with alphabet Σ = {a, b} is given below.


Which of the following finite state machines is a valid minimal DFA which accepts the same language as D?

[GATE 2011|| MCQ|| 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
In  (B)  and  (C)  when the first letter of input is 'b' we reach final state, while in the given  DFA  first letter 'b' is not a final state. So,  (B)  and  (C)  are not accepting same language as the given  DFA.
In  (D)  we can reach final state when the last letter is 'a', whatever be the previous transitions. But in the given  DFA , when the first 2 letters are 'b' we can never reach the final state. So,  (D)  is also accepting a different language than the given  DFA.

questions.question:
Consider the set of strings on {0,1} in which, every substring of 3 symbols has at most two zeros. For example, 001110 and 011001 are in the language, but 100010 is not. All strings of length less than 3 are also in the language. A partially completed DFA that accepts this language is shown below.
. The missing arcs in the DFA are:
[GATE 2012|| MCQ|| 2-mark]
questions.question_html:
Consider the set of strings on {0,1} in which, every substring of 3 symbols has at most two zeros. For example, 001110 and 011001 are in the language, but 100010 is not. All strings of length less than 3 are also in the language. A partially completed DFA that accepts this language is shown below.

The missing arcs in the DFA are:

[GATE 2012|| MCQ|| 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
Except for “q,” which is a trap state, all states are final. Language strings are constructed in such a way that every substring of three symbols contains no more than two zeros. It signifies that there can't be three zeros in a row anywhere in the string. Total four transitions are missing in the given DFA, thus we must design them while keeping in mind the requirement that "three consecutive zeros" will lead to trap state "q."
From the state “00” it is clear that if another “0” comes then the string is going to be rejected, so from state “00” the transition with input “0” will lead to state “q”. So option A and B are eliminated.
Option C now includes a self loop of "0" on state "10," which accepts any number of zeros (including more than three zeros), therefore it is likewise incorrect. We were only left with option D, which is the correct choice.

Option C now includes a self loop of "0" on state "10," which accepts any number of zeros (including more than three zeros), therefore it is likewise incorrect. We were only left with option D, which is the correct choice.

The DFA recognises the phrase "all strings ending in 1."

.. The following grammar can yield the strings "abab," "aaab," and "abbaa."

Statement I: L1 = {0 ^p 1 ^q | p, q ∈ N},
Regular expression: 0⁺1⁺
Any number of 0's followed by any number of 1's. L1 requires no stack. Hence L1 is regular.
Every regular language is a context-free language.
Statement II: L2 = {0 ^p 1 ^q | p, q ∈ N and p = q}
Context-Free Language: L2 = {0 ^p 1 ^p | p ∈ N}
The number of 0's is equal to the number of 1's. L2 requires only one stack. Hence L2 is context-free language.
Statement III: L3 = {0 ^p 1 ^q 0 ^r | p, q, r ∈ N and p = q = r}
Context-Sensitive Language: L3 = {0 ^p 1 ^p 0 ^p | p ∈ N}.

questions.explanation:
In this PDA, we can give labels to states as q₀, q₁, q₂
where q₀ and q₂ are final states.
This PDA accepts the string in two ways —
i.e., by using q₀ as a final state and by using q₂ it accepts as an empty stack.
Since q₀ is the initial as well as the final state, it can accept any number of a’s (including zero a’s).
By using q₂ as the empty stack, it accepts strings that have an equal number of a’s and b’s (b’s come after a’s).
Hence, L = {aⁿ | n ≥ 0} ∪ {aⁿbⁿ | n ≥ 0}.

questions.question_html:
Consider the following languages:
L1 = {anbmcn+m: m, n > 1}
L2 = {anbnc2n: n > 1}
Which one of the following is TRUE?

[GATE 2016||SET-2 MCQ|| 2-mark]

options.text: Both L1 and L2 are context-free.
options.html: Both L1 and L2 are context-free.

options.text: L1 is context-free while L2 is not context-free.
options.html: L1 is context-free while L2 is not context-free.

options.html: L2 is context-free while L1 is not context-free.

options.text: Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.
options.html: Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free.

questions.explanation:
Statement I: L₁ = {aⁿbᵐcⁿ⁺ᵐ : m, n ≥ 1}
L₁ can be accepted easily by a single stack.
First, push a’s into the stack, then push b’s into the stack, then read c’s and pop b’s.
When no b’s are left on the stack, keep reading c’s and pop a’s.
When no c’s are left in the input and the stack is empty, then the string is accepted by one stack.
Hence, L₁ is a context-free language.
Statement II: L₂ = {a ^ⁿ b ^ⁿ c ^2n : n ≥ 1}
This language can’t be accepted by a single stack.
When we push a’s into the stack and pop them with b’s, it satisfies the condition that the number of a’s = number of b’s, but we are left with an empty stack and c’s in the input.
We can’t ensure that the number of c’s is double that of a’s and b’s.
A pushdown automaton can’t remember the number of a’s for the third set of characters, i.e., c’s.
Hence, L₂ is a context-sensitive language but not a context-free language.
questions.explanation_html:
Statement I: L₁ = {aⁿbᵐcⁿ⁺ᵐ : m, n ≥ 1}

L₁ can be accepted easily by a single stack.
First, push a’s into the stack, then push b’s into the stack, then read c’s and pop b’s.
When no b’s are left on the stack, keep reading c’s and pop a’s.
When no c’s are left in the input and the stack is empty, then the string is accepted by one stack.
Hence, L₁ is a context-free language.

Statement II: L₂ = {aⁿbⁿc2n : n ≥ 1}

This language can’t be accepted by a single stack.
When we push a’s into the stack and pop them with b’s, it satisfies the condition that the number of a’s = number of b’s, but we are left with an empty stack and c’s in the input.
We can’t ensure that the number of c’s is double that of a’s and b’s.
A pushdown automaton can’t remember the number of a’s for the third set of characters, i.e., c’s.

Hence, L₂ is a context-sensitive language but not a context-free language.

L₁ = {aᵖ | p is a prime number}

Option 2:
L₂ = {aⁿbᵐc²ᵐ | n ≥ 0, m ≥ 0}

It can be solved easily using single stack. Comparison is done between b and c. We can make a pushdown automaton for this. So, L₂ is context-free language.

Option 3:

First comparison is between a and b, then between b and c, therefore two stacks are required.

So, it is context-sensitive language. L₃ is recursive language.

First push a into a stack then pop a for each b from the stack. The given language is accepted by DPDA

3. If L is regular language, then, is L'(complement of L) also regular?

4. If L is recursive language, then, is L'(complement of L) also recursive?

[GATE 2012|| MCQ|| 1-mark]
questions.explanation:
1) This is a variation of Turing Machine Halting problem and it is undecidable.
2)CFL are not closed under complement so it is undecidable.
3) Complement of Regular languages is also regular. Then a DFA that accepts the complement of L, i.e. ∑* – L, can be obtained by swapping its accepting states with its non-accepting states. This is decidable.
4) Recursive language are closed under complement, so it is decidable.

questions.explanation:
option A is correct
{wxxᴿwᴿ | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*} is context-free language as it can be done using a stack. here is the way
- push all letters of w in the stack
- push all letters of x in the stack
- if a new letter matches with the top of the stack, pop it
at the end stack will be empty so, it is context free language
option B is correct
{wxwᴿ | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*} is context-free.
push all the letters before w in the stack, skip x, now pop an element from the stack for each new letter, (top of the stack will match with each new letter due to wᴿ). So, it is context-free
option C is incorrect
{wxwᴿxᴿ | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*} is Not context-free, we have to match w with wᴿwᴿ but the top of the stack contains x, so not possible.
option D is correct
{wwᴿxxᴿ| w, x ∈ {0, 1}*} is Context-free language,
first match w with wᴿ then match x with xᴿ using a stack.
option 1,2,4 are correct.

The regular expression can be written for the language L₁.

L₁ is regular because by putting n=0, we create a subset {w | w ∈ {a,b}} which contains all possible strings. So if the subset of L₁ is (a + b), then L₁ = (a + b)* which is regular.

L₂ = {wxwʳ | w, x ∈ {a, b}*, |w|, |x| > 0}

The regular expression can be written for the language L₂.

L₂ is regular because by putting w as "a" and "b" we get a regular expression
a(a+b)a + b(a+b)b, which covers all other strings which can be obtained by putting w as "aa", "ab", "ba" and "bb", etc.

So L₂ = a(a+b)a + b(a+b)b which is regular.

Now every regular is also context-free.

questions.explanation:

4. True. Recursively enumerable languages are closed under intersection — dovetail the two enumerators/simulators and accept when both machines accept.

𝑟 = 0 ∗ + 1 ∗

The total number of strings of length less than or equal to 5, which are neither in 𝑟 nor in 𝑠, is _________

[GATE 2024||SET-1 NAT|| 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
For strings of length 2 :
{00, 11} are in r (all zeros or all ones).
{01, 10} are in s (start with 0 followed by 1 or start with 1 followed by 0).
For strings of length 3 :
{000, 111} are in r (all zeros or all ones).
{011, 100} are in s (start with 0 followed by ones or start with 1 followed by zeros).
For strings of length 4 :
{0000, 1111} are in r (all zeros or all ones).
{0111, 1000} are in s (start with 0 followed by ones or start with 1 followed by zeros).
For strings of length 5 :
{00000, 11111} are in r (all zeros or all ones).
{01111, 10000} are in s (start with 0 followed by ones or start with 1 followed by zeros).
Next, we calculate the number of strings not in r and not in s for each string length and sum them.
= (2² − 4) + (2³ − 4) + (2⁴ − 4) + (2⁵ − 4)
= (4 − 4) + (8 − 4) + (16 − 4) + (32 − 4)
= 0 + 4 + 12 + 28 = 44
Thus, the total number of strings of length less than or equal to 5, which are neither in r nor in s , is 44 .

questions.explanation:
NFA → DFA conversion:
When we convert an NFA to an equivalent DFA,
the DFA can have up to 2ⁿ states , because each DFA state represents a subset of NFA states.
So, saying “DFA has at most n states” is wrong .
Complement of L:
- To take the complement , we need a complete DFA (because we just flip final and non-final states).
- For an NFA, complementing directly is not possible — you must convert it to DFA first.
So saying “complement can be accepted by an NFA with at most n states” is wrong .
- Regular expression:
Every NFA represents a regular language , and every regular language can be written as a regular expression — that is true .
- DFA with 2ⁿ states:
The subset construction guarantees that a DFA with ≤ 2ⁿ states always exists — that is true .

  4. DFA with 2ⁿ states:
    The subset construction guarantees that a DFA with ≤ 2ⁿ states always exists — that is true.

questions.question:
Let 𝑆 be the set of all ternary strings defined over the alphabet {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. Consider all strings in 𝑆 that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols, that is, “aa”, “bb” or “cc”. The number of such strings of length 5 that are possible is _______. (Answer in integer)
[GATE 2025||SET-1 NAT|| 1-mark]
questions.question_html:
Let 𝑆 be the set of all ternary strings defined over the alphabet {𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐}. Consider all strings in 𝑆 that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols, that is, “aa”, “bb” or “cc”. The number of such strings of length 5 that are possible is _______. (Answer in integer)

[GATE 2025||SET-1 NAT|| 1-mark]
questions.explanation:
To solve this problem, we need to count the number of ternary strings of length 5 over the alphabet {a,b,c} that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols (i.e., "aa", "bb", or "cc"). We will use the  complementary counting  approach:
- Total number of ternary strings of length 5: - Each position in the string can be filled with a, b, or c.
- Total strings = 3 ^5  = 243.
- Number of ternary strings of length 5 with no two consecutive symbols: - For the first position, there are 3 choices (a, b, or c).
- For each subsequent position, there are 2 choices (any symbol except the one used in the previous position).
- Total strings with no two consecutive symbols = 3 × 2 ^4  = 3 × 16 = 48.
- Number of ternary strings of length 5 with at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols: - This is the complement of the above case.
- Total strings with at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols = Total strings - Strings with no two consecutive symbols.
- Total = 243 − 48 = 195.
The number of such strings of length 5 is: 195
questions.explanation_html:
To solve this problem, we need to count the number of ternary strings of length 5 over the alphabet {a,b,c} that contain at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols (i.e., "aa", "bb", or "cc"). We will use the complementary counting approach:

  1. Total number of ternary strings of length 5:
    • Each position in the string can be filled with a, b, or c.
    • Total strings = 35 = 243.
  2. Number of ternary strings of length 5 with no two consecutive symbols:
    • For the first position, there are 3 choices (a, b, or c).
    • For each subsequent position, there are 2 choices (any symbol except the one used in the previous position).
    • Total strings with no two consecutive symbols = 3 × 24 = 3 × 16 = 48.
  3. Number of ternary strings of length 5 with at least one occurrence of two consecutive symbols:

questions.explanation:
For L1:
The language requires that the number of a's and b's must be equal (aᵐbᵐ), and the number of c's must be greater than or equal to the number of a's (cᵐ⁺ⁿ).
This involves two simultaneous conditions or comparisons: one for equality between the number of a's and b's, and another for comparing the number of a's to c's.
Context-Free Grammars (CFGs) cannot handle two conditions at once, which require comparison of counts, thus, L₁ cannot be generated by a context-free grammar.
Therefore, L₁ is not a context-free language.
For L₂:
The language structure {aᵐbⁿcᵐ⁺ⁿ} allows for:
- Pushing a's onto a stack.
- Pushing b's onto the same stack until c appears.
- Matching c's with a's and b's as they pop off the stack.
This behavior can be captured using a Pushdown Automaton (PDA), which aligns with how context-free languages are processed.
Thus, L₂ is a context-free language.

Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?
[GATE 2025||SET-2 MCQ|| 1-mark]
questions.question_html:
Let 𝐺1, 𝐺2 be Context Free Grammars (CFGs) and 𝑅 be a regular expression. For a grammar 𝐺, let 𝐿(𝐺) denote the language generated by 𝐺.
Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?

[GATE 2025||SET-2 MCQ|| 1-mark]
questions.explanation:
1. Is L(G1) = L(G2)?
Undecidable. Determining if two context-free grammars (CFGs) generate the same language is undecidable. There is no algorithm that can decide whether two arbitrary CFGs generate the same language.
2. Is L(G1) ∩ L(G2) = ∅?
Undecidable. The intersection of two context-free languages is not necessarily context-free, and checking if two context-free languages have an empty intersection is undecidable. This is a generalization of the undecidability of the equivalence problem for CFGs.
3. Is L(G1) = L(R)?
Undecidable. The equivalence of a context-free language (generated by a CFG) and a regular language (defined by a regular expression) is undecidable. Determining whether a CFG generates a language that is exactly the same as a regular language is undecidable.
4. Is L(G1) = ∅?
Decidable. It is decidable whether the language generated by a context-free grammar G1 is empty. This can be determined by analyzing the grammar to check if there exists a derivation for any string. Specifically, this can be done by checking if there is any derivation starting from the start symbol and reaching a terminal string.

Let 𝐿1 be the language represented by the regular expression 𝑏 ∗𝑎𝑏 ∗ (𝑎𝑏 ∗𝑎𝑏 ∗ ) ∗ and 𝐿2 = { 𝑤 ∈ (𝑎 + 𝑏) ∗ | |𝑤| ≤ 4}, where |𝑤| denotes the length of string 𝑤. The number of strings in 𝐿2 which are also in 𝐿1 is ___________

[GATE 2024||SET-2 NAT|| 2-mark]
questions.explanation:
L₁ is exactly the strings with an odd number of a’s (at least one).
For length n ≥ 1, half of the 2ⁿ binary strings have an odd number of a’s, i.e. 2 ^n-1
So for lengths 1 to 4: 2 ^0 + 2¹ + 2² + 2³ = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15.

questions.title: Context free language and context free grammar|| (1 Mark)||MSQ||2022
questions.question_html:
Consider the following languages: 

L1 = {ww | w ∈ {a, b}* }

L2 = {anbncm | m, n≥ 0}

L3 = {ambncn | m, n≥ 0}

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

[GATE 2022|| MSQ|| 1-mark]

options.text: L _1  is not context-free but L _2  and L _3  are deterministic context-free.
Neither L _1  nor L _2  is context-free.
L _2 , L _3 , _  and L _2  ∩ L _3  all are context-free.
Neither L _1  nor its complement is context-free.

questions.explanation:
L1 is not context-free but L2 and L3 are deterministic context-free. True ,
L1 is not context-free and L2 and L3 are clearly DCFLs since they have only one comparison and DPDA can accept both.
Neither L1 nor L2 is context-free - False
L2 is DCFL and every DCFL is a CFL.
L2 - Context-free.
L3 - Context-free.
L2 ∩ L3 = {aⁿbⁿcⁿ or aᵐbᵐcᵐ , m,n ≥ 0}
This language is context-sensitive language.
L2, L3 and L2 ∩ L3 all are context-free. - False
L2 - Context-free.
L3 - Context-free.
L2 ∩ L3 = {aⁿbⁿcⁿ, n ≥ 0} We cannot compare aⁿbⁿcⁿ with one single stack. Hence it is not context-free.
Neither L1 nor its complement is context-free.
False , L1 - Not context-free.
L1′ (Complement of L1) is accepted by a context-free grammar, hence it is CFL.

questions.title: ISRO _CS_2025 ||Theory of Computation || Context-Free Grammar (CFG) || Question 56
questions.question:
Consider the context-free grammar G below. Here S is the starting non-terminal symbol, while a and b are terminal symbols.
S → a S b | T
T → b T | b
The language generated by G is:
questions.question_html:
Consider the context-free grammar G below. Here S is the starting non-terminal symbol, while a and b are terminal symbols.

S → a S b | T

T → b T | b

The language generated by G is:
questions.explanation:
- T→bT∣b generates b ^n with n≥1.
- S→aSb∣T applies the aSb rule m times (possibly zero), then uses T. That yields strings of the form
a ^m b ^n b ^m =a ^m b ^n+m with m≥0 and n≥1.
- Let k=n+m. Then k>m, so the language is all a ^m b ^k with 0≤m<k, i.e. 0≤m<n if we write the exponent of b as n. Hence option (a).
questions.explanation_html:

  • Let k=n+m. Then k>m, so the language is all ambk with 0≤m<k, i.e. 0≤m<n if we write the exponent of b as n. Hence option (a).
